meta.title: Java Reference
meta.subtitle: Foundations to Expert · Java 25 LTS
meta.renderNote: Canonical source. HTML and PDF are generated FROM this file. Edit here, regenerate both. Never edit the outputs directly.

typeVocabulary.concept: A mechanism or idea — how something works and why.
typeVocabulary.syntax: A concrete form, keyword, or tool invocation.

provenance.verified: Every entry is drawn from the learner's actual completed lessons (through Lesson 1.3) and its outputs verified against a real JDK 21.

topicAreas.name: Language & Runtime
topicAreas.glance: Java source is compiled once by javac into platform-neutral bytecode, then run on any machine by the java launcher and its JVM. The JVM — not the compiler — produces the final machine code, and it runs any .class file, so other JVM languages share the same runtime. This area also covers the everyday toolchain: the edit-compile-run loop, javap, comments, the class/file-name rule, and how OpenJDK becomes an installable distribution like Temurin.

entries.essence: Java source is compiled to platform-neutral bytecode, which the JVM then executes at run time — so the same compiled program runs on any machine with a JVM.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: javac turns Foo.java into Foo.class (bytecode, not machine code). java starts the JVM, which loads that bytecode and — via JIT compilation and interpretation — turns it into native machine code as the program runs.

detail.label: Why
detail.body: The bytecode layer is the portability seam: compile once, and any operating system with a JVM can run the result. That is the whole 'write once, run anywhere' claim, made concrete.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
javac Hello.java   # source  -> Hello.class (bytecode)
java Hello         # JVM loads bytecode -> runs it

entries.essence: The compiler. It reads a .java source file and produces a .class bytecode file.

detail.label: Form
detail.body: javac takes the full file name, extension included.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
javac Hello.java   # requires the .java extension
# produces: Hello.class

entries.essence: The launcher. It starts the JVM on a compiled class and runs its main method.

detail.label: Form
detail.body: java takes the class NAME only — no .java, no .class extension.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: The final step from bytecode to native machine code happens HERE, inside the JVM — not in javac. javac stops at bytecode.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
java Hello   # bare class name, NO extension
# NOT: java Hello.class  (wrong)

entries.essence: Three nested layers: the JDK builds programs, the JRE runs them, and the JVM inside it actually executes the bytecode.

detail.label: The three layers
facts: JDK
JRE + developer tools (javac, javap, jar). What you install to WRITE Java.
JRE
JVM + the standard class libraries. Enough to RUN a finished program.
JVM
the engine inside that actually executes the bytecode.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: You need the JDK to write and compile Java. A machine that only runs finished Java programs needs just the runtime.

entries.essence: Platform-neutral instructions stored in .class files — the JVM's 'machine code', not the CPU's.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: javac emits bytecode: a compact, CPU-independent instruction set the JVM understands. The JVM translates it to real machine instructions at run time via interpretation and JIT compilation. It is the JVM's instruction set, not any physical processor's.

detail.label: Two forms
facts: mnemonics
human-readable ops printed by javap -c (getstatic, invokevirtual, ldc).
raw bytes
the numeric encoding of those ops stored in the .class file.

detail.label: Why it matters
detail.body: Because the JVM runs any valid .class file, not Java specifically, other languages that compile to bytecode — Kotlin, Scala, Clojure, Groovy — run on the same JVM unchanged. Bytecode, not the Java language, is the portability seam.

entries.essence: The fixed method signature the JVM calls to start a program — public static void main(String[] args).

detail.label: Form
Each keyword
facts: public
the JVM must be able to call it from outside the class
static
it runs without first creating an object of the class
void
it returns nothing to the JVM
main
the exact name the JVM looks for as the entry point
String[] args
command-line arguments, passed in as an array of strings

detail.label: Note
detail.body: Java 25 adds a simplified entry point (an instance main, no class or args boilerplate) as a preview. This course writes the classic signature above — it is the stable, universal form and what the OCP exam tests.

entries.essence: After editing a .java file you must re-run javac before java, or the JVM keeps running the previous compiled version.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: java executes the .class file, never the .java source. Editing the source does not change the .class until javac rebuilds it. Skip the recompile and you silently run stale bytecode.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: Every code change: save, javac, then java. If a change seems to have no effect, an un-rerun javac is the first thing to check.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
# edit Hello.java, then ALWAYS:
javac Hello.java   # rebuild the .class
java Hello         # now runs the new version

entries.essence: The disassembler — prints the bytecode instructions inside a compiled .class file in readable form.

detail.label: Form
detail.body: javap -c takes a class name and prints its methods as bytecode mnemonics. It reads the compiled class, not the source.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: Confirming what the compiler actually produced — seeing that source became bytecode, and what those instructions are.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
javap -c Hello   # prints Hello.class bytecode
# e.g. getstatic, ldc, invokevirtual, return

entries.essence: Notes ignored by the compiler — line comments with //, block comments with /* */, and Javadoc with /** */.

detail.label: Form
rows: Javadoc — documents the class/method that follows

detail.label: Note
detail.body: All three are stripped before compilation and have no effect on the running program. Javadoc /** */ is additionally read by the javadoc tool to generate API docs.

entries.essence: public class Name { ... } — and a public class must live in a file named exactly Name.java.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: A source file's public class must share the file's name, character for character. Hello.java must declare public class Hello; a mismatch is a compile error.

detail.label: Form
Note
detail.body: The name is case-sensitive: Hello and hello are different. This is why javac takes the file name (Hello.java) but java takes the class name (Hello).

entries.essence: OpenJDK is the open-source reference codebase; vendors build it into installable distributions such as Eclipse Temurin (from Adoptium).

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: OpenJDK is the shared source. Multiple vendors compile it into ready-to-install JDKs that pass the same compatibility tests, so they behave identically for standard Java. They differ in packaging, support terms, and update cadence, not language behavior.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: Choosing what to install: a free, well-supported build like Temurin (Adoptium) is a standard choice. This course uses Temurin JDK 25 LTS (x64 = the 64-bit build).

detail.label: Note
detail.body: LTS (Long-Term Support) releases receive updates for years; Java 25 is an LTS. Non-LTS releases are superseded every six months.

topicAreas.name: Types & Variables
topicAreas.glance: Java has eight primitive value types with fixed, machine-independent sizes, plus a matching wrapper class for each. A bare whole-number literal is an int and a bare decimal is a double; suffixes (L, f) and the overflow rule follow from that. Primitives are held and copied by value, which is the root of how == later behaves on objects versus primitives.

entries.essence: The eight built-in value types, with fixed sizes identical on every machine, stored directly rather than as objects.

detail.label: The eight primitives
rows: IEEE 754 single precision, about 6-7 significant digits
IEEE 754 double precision, about 15-16 significant digits

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: A primitive variable holds the value itself, in its own fixed-size slot — not a reference to an object elsewhere. The sizes above are the same on every platform, so integer overflow and float precision behave identically everywhere.

detail.label: Note
detail.body: int is the default type for whole numbers and double for decimals; the integer types are signed two's-complement, and char is the one unsigned integer type.

entries.essence: A 32-bit signed whole number — Java's default type for integer values.

detail.label: Range
detail.body: -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647 (-2^31 to 2^31-1), stored as 4 bytes two's-complement.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: Counting, indexing, and whole-number arithmetic that stays in range. Use long when a value may exceed the int range — exceeding it wraps around silently rather than erroring.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
int count = 42;
int sum = a + b;

entries.essence: A 64-bit IEEE 754 floating-point number — Java's default type for decimal values.

detail.label: Range
detail.body: About 15-16 significant decimal digits. Stores a very wide range of magnitudes, but as binary fractions, so some decimals (like 0.1) are held approximately.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: Any calculation with fractional values where tiny rounding error is acceptable — averages, measurements, graphics. Avoid it for exact money math.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
double g = 9.81;
double avg = total / (double) n;

entries.essence: An object class matching each primitive (int -> Integer, etc.), used where Java needs an object instead of a raw value.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: Anywhere an object is required but a primitive is not allowed — most importantly the collections classes taught later. The wrappers also hold useful constants and helpers, e.g. Integer.MAX_VALUE and Integer.parseInt("42").

detail.label: Note
detail.body: Two of the class names differ from the primitive: int -> Integer and char -> Character. All eight live in java.lang, so no import is needed.

entries.essence: A bare whole number is typed int and a bare decimal is typed double; suffixes L and f change that, and underscores group digits.

detail.label: The overflow trap
detail.body: long big = 10000000000; does NOT compile. The literal is typed int before it is ever assigned to the long, and 10,000,000,000 overflows int's range — so it fails at the literal itself. Write 10_000_000_000L so it is a long from the start.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: A suffix sets the literal's type up front; it does not convert an already-typed value. Underscores may sit only between digits, never at the start, end, or next to the decimal point.

entries.essence: Name a typed storage slot and optionally give it a value: type name = value;

detail.label: Form
Note
detail.body: The declared type fixes what the variable can hold for its whole life. A declaration without a value (int count;) is legal, but the variable must be assigned before it is read.

entries.essence: Lets the compiler infer a LOCAL variable's type from its initializer — still fully static typing, just written shorter.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: var total = 0.0; compiles exactly as double total = 0.0; — the compiler reads the initializer, fixes the type permanently, and var vanishes. The variable is not dynamically typed and cannot later hold a different type.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: A local variable whose type is obvious from the right-hand side. var requires an initializer, so the compiler has something to infer from.

detail.label: Cannot be used for
detail.body: Fields, method parameters, return types, or a declaration with no initializer (var x; is a compile error) — none of these give the compiler an initializer to read.

entries.essence: Assigning or passing a primitive copies its value, so the copy and the original are fully independent afterward.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: A primitive variable holds its value directly. b = a copies a's value into b's own slot; changing one afterward never affects the other.

detail.label: Example
Why it matters
detail.body: This is the contrast that makes reference types (objects) understandable later: those copy a reference to one shared object, not the object itself. The difference is exactly what == will hinge on.

topicAreas.name: Operators, Casting & Math
topicAreas.glance: Integer division truncates and % gives the remainder; mixing in one floating-point operand promotes the whole expression to floating point. Widening to a bigger type is automatic, narrowing to a smaller one needs an explicit cast that truncates toward zero, with a compile-time-constant exception. The Math methods have fixed return types worth memorizing — pow and sqrt always return double, round returns an integer type.

entries.essence: When both operands are integers, / discards the fractional part and % returns the remainder.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: int / int produces an int: the mathematical result is truncated toward zero, not rounded. % returns what is left over after that truncated division.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: This is the classic surprise: dividing two ints can never yield a decimal. Make one operand floating-point to get a real quotient (see numeric promotion).

entries.essence: In a mixed-type arithmetic expression, the narrower operand is promoted so both share the wider type before the operation runs.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: If either operand is a floating-point type, the whole operation is done in floating point. So 9.0 / 4 promotes 4 to 4.0 and yields 2.25, whereas 9 / 4 is pure int division and yields 2.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: To force a real quotient from two int variables, make one a double first: (double) a / b.

entries.essence: Widening to a larger type is automatic; narrowing to a smaller type needs an explicit (type) cast and can lose data.

detail.label: Widening (automatic)
detail.body: A smaller type flows into a larger one with no cast, because no information is lost: int -> long -> double, for example. int x = 5; double d = x; is fine.

detail.label: Narrowing (explicit)
detail.body: Going to a smaller type requires a cast and may lose data. A floating-to-integer cast truncates toward zero — it does not round: (int) 9.99 is 9 and (int) -9.99 is -9.

detail.label: Example

entries.essence: A final variable holding a compile-time constant that fits the target can be assigned to a narrower type WITHOUT a cast.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: When the right-hand side is a final variable whose constant value is known at compile time and fits the smaller type's range, the compiler allows the assignment with no cast. Drop final (or exceed the range) and it becomes a normal narrowing that fails to compile.

detail.label: Example
Use when
detail.body: Mostly an exam and edge-case detail — it explains why an apparently identical assignment compiles in one place and not another.

entries.essence: The java.lang.Math helpers have fixed return types worth memorizing — notably pow/sqrt always return double and round returns an integer type.

detail.label: Note
detail.body: pow and sqrt return double even when the result is a whole number, so Math.pow(2,10) is 1024.0, not 1024. round is the one that hands back an integer type: long for a double argument, int for a float argument.

topicAreas.name: Strings & Text
topicAreas.glance: A String is an immutable object — every method that seems to change it actually returns a new one. That single fact explains the literal pool, why == is the classic trap for anyone coming from Python, and why StringBuilder exists. Text blocks add readable multi-line literals, with precise, easy-to-miss whitespace rules.

entries.essence: An immutable sequence of characters (UTF-16) — a reference type, not a primitive, with special literal and + syntax.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: A String is an object holding a fixed character sequence. The language gives it two conveniences no other class gets: double-quote literals ("hi") and the + concatenation operator.

detail.label: Example
detail.code:
String s = "hello";
String greeting = s + ", world";

entries.essence: Once a String exists, its contents can never change — every 'modifying' method returns a brand-new String and leaves the original untouched.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: Methods like toUpperCase(), trim(), and replace() do not edit the String in place. They compute and return a new String object; the original is unchanged.

detail.label: Proof
detail.body: Verified empirically in-lesson: calling s.toUpperCase() and then re-checking s showed s was untouched — the uppercased text only exists in the returned value.

detail.label: Why it matters
detail.body: Immutability is what makes the literal pool and thread-safety possible — a shared String can never be altered out from under another user of it.

detail.label: Example

entries.essence: Identical String literals share one single object in a cache called the pool — safe only because Strings can never change.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: When the same literal text appears more than once, Java reuses one pooled object rather than making duplicates. Because Strings are immutable, sharing is safe — no user of the shared object can mutate it.

detail.label: Consequence
detail.body: Two identical literals are literally the same object, so == between them happens to return true. This is an implementation detail — never rely on it for comparison.

entries.essence: Explicitly constructs a fresh String object that deliberately does NOT come from the pool — even if an identical literal already exists.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: new String("a") forces a distinct object. So new String("a") == "a" is false (different objects), even though their contents are equal.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: Almost never in normal code. Its main value is illustrating the pool: it is the deliberate way to opt out of literal sharing.

detail.label: Example

entries.essence: == asks 'are these the same object?' (reference identity); .equals() asks 'do these have the same contents?' — for Strings, always use .equals().

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: == compares references: are both variables pointing at the exact same object? .equals() (as String overrides it) compares the actual character sequences.

detail.label: The Python trap
detail.body: In Python, == compares values. In Java, == on any object compares identity, not value. Carrying the Python habit over is the classic source of String bugs.

detail.label: Example

entries.essence: A mutable character buffer — the right tool for building a String piece by piece, because it edits one buffer in place instead of creating a new object per step.

detail.label: Mechanism
detail.body: StringBuilder holds a growable internal buffer (initial capacity 16). append() and insert() modify that one buffer; you call toString() at the end to get the finished String.

detail.label: Use when
detail.body: Assembling text in a loop, or from many pieces. For a couple of + operations, plain String is fine and clearer.

detail.label: Example

entries.essence: Multi-line String literals delimited by triple quotes — readable JSON, HTML, or SQL without escaped newlines.

detail.label: Form
detail.body: Open with """ followed immediately by a newline; write the content on following lines; close with """. The result is an ordinary String.

entries.essence: Text blocks strip the whitespace you didn't mean and keep the whitespace you did — via a few precise rules worth memorizing.

detail.label: Incidental leading whitespace
detail.body: The common indentation shared by all lines (measured against the least-indented line and the closing """ position) is stripped automatically, so you can indent the block to match your code without that indentation appearing in the string.

detail.label: Trailing whitespace
detail.body: Whitespace at the end of each line is removed automatically.

detail.label: Keep a trailing space: \s
detail.body: End a line with the \s escape to preserve exactly one trailing space that would otherwise be stripped.

detail.label: Join lines: trailing \
detail.body: End a line with a backslash to suppress its newline — the next line continues the same logical line.
